title: Stack vs heap
instruction:
Two places memory lives:

**Stack**: local variables, function parameters — automatic allocation/deallocation, fast, limited size (~1MB default).
**Heap**: dynamically allocated with `new` — manual, large, slower.

Most variables go on the stack. Large data, dynamic-size data, and objects that outlive their function go on the heap.
successMessage: Stack variables are freed automatically when they go out of scope. Fast and safe — but limited to about 1MB on most systems. For large data: use the heap via new or vector (which manages heap internally).

title: new and delete — manual heap allocation
instruction:
`new` allocates memory on the heap and returns a pointer.
`delete` frees it.

**Golden rule**: every `new` must have exactly one matching `delete`.
successMessage: delete for single objects, delete[] for arrays. Never mix them. After deleting, set to nullptr — using a pointer after delete is 'use-after-free' — undefined behaviour that causes security vulnerabilities.

title: Memory leaks
instruction:
A **memory leak** happens when you `new` but forget to `delete`.

Leaks slowly drain available memory — the program works fine at first, then crashes or slows after hours of running.

The fixed version shows how to avoid the leak.
successMessage: Calling leakyFunction() 1000 times leaks 400KB. In a server running for months, that adds up to crashes. Manual delete works but smart pointers (next step) eliminate leaks entirely.

title: unique_ptr — exclusive ownership
instruction:
`unique_ptr` automatically deletes when it goes out of scope. Zero overhead vs raw pointer.

```cpp
auto p = make_unique<int>(42);  // allocated
// p is automatically deleted when it leaves scope!
```

`unique_ptr` can't be copied — only one owner at a time. Transfer with `move()`.
successMessage: The memory was freed automatically when the block ended — no delete needed. make_unique is the modern way: no raw new, no raw delete, no leaks. This is RAII: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization.

title: shared_ptr — shared ownership
instruction:
`shared_ptr` allows multiple owners. It tracks the reference count — deletes when the last owner goes away.

Use when multiple parts of the code need to own the same object.
successMessage: The Resource was created once and destroyed once — exactly right. use_count() tracks owners. shared_ptr is perfect for scene graphs, shared caches, and any object with multiple owners.

title: Build a safe resource manager
instruction:
No starter code.

Create a `Buffer` struct with:
- A `unique_ptr<int[]>` for data
- A `size` field
- Constructor that allocates the buffer
- `set(int index, int value)` and `get(int index)` with bounds checking
- Destructor that prints "Buffer freed."

Create two Buffers, set some values, print them. Verify the destructor message appears automatically.
hint: struct Buffer { int size; unique_ptr<int[]> data; Buffer(int n) : size(n), data(make_unique<int[]>(n)) {} ... };

codeChecks.message: Use unique_ptr<int[]> for the buffer data.
Define a destructor that prints 'Buffer freed.'
Add bounds checking in set() and get().

successMessage: A safe buffer that never leaks — unique_ptr ensures automatic cleanup. The destructor print confirms when memory is released. RAII in action. Chapter 10 done!
